summary: DHH reflects on his complicated relationship with Instagram, describing how he returned to the platform in 2015 and found it transformed into a curated showcase of perfect lives. Despite being self-aware about the highlight-reel nature of the platform, he found himself trapped in addictive patterns of posting and checking for validation. He acknowledges his own complicity in projecting an unrealistic lifestyle of race cars, travel, and glamour. Prompted by ongoing Facebook controversies, he decided to leave again, recognizing that what you consume visually colors your thoughts and ultimately your soul.
key_points: Instagram evolved from casual phone photos into an intoxicating stream of curated perfection that leaves users perpetually hungry for more
Self-awareness about Instagram's unreality provides no real defense against its addictive feedback loops of posting and seeking validation
DHH admits complicity in feeding an aesthetic that devours unfulfilled aspirations and produces alienation
The messenger's 'last active' timestamps revealed that most people on his list were similarly hooked, shattering the illusion of personal weakness
Paraphrasing Marcus Aurelius: what you watch colors your soul, making visual consumption a matter of spiritual hygiene
money_quotes: You don't really realize how cooked you're getting while sitting in the stew. But something important is dissolving.
Chasing affection from people I mostly didn't know, so I could please an ego that vowed not to care.

I've contributed to an utterly unrealistic and unhealthy depiction of success or even just The Good Life. I've feed an aesthetic that devours unfulfilled aspirations and spits out alienation and resignation.
The aesthetic becomes the essence. And the feedback loop sends you in circles. It's addictive. Even when you're aware of it, even when you have your guard up.
You'd think there'd only be so many cars or watches or beaches or sunsets or whatever you could take in before you're full. But somehow Instagram always just left me hungry for more.
The main ingredients in credibility is time and commitment. Otherwise it easily just comes off as obnoxious.
You probably have a completely healthy relationship with the lifestyle projections and Good Life aesthetics you're consuming on Instagram. Right? Of course you do.
key_insight: Self-awareness about social media's curated unreality offers no real protection against its addictive pull, because the aesthetic seeps in and colors your soul regardless of your intellectual defenses.
